texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has stated that in order to effectively address global warming and climate change as outlined in the Paris Agreement, there must be a significant decrease in global net anthropogenic CO2 emissions. Specifically, emissions must be reduced by approximately 45% from their 2010 levels by 2030 and brought to net zero around 2050 in order to limit the global temperature increase to 1.5 ◦ C [1]. As projected by the United States Energy Information Administration (EIA), global energy consumption is forecasted to experience a substantial uptick of almost 50% by 2050, compared to the levels observed in 2020 [2]. Despite the projected increase in the utilization of renewable energy sources, it is estimated that fossil fuels will continue to comprise approximately 70% of the world ' s total energy consumption in 2050, due in part to the anticipated population growth.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymeric facilitated transport membranes (FTMs) that contain amino acid salts (AASs) provide a cost-effective option for reducing the CO2 emissions associated with burning fossil fuels. In the current work, we examined the effects of CO2  partial pressure and temperature on the CO2  absorption properties of three AAS mobile carriers, piperazine  glycinate  (PZ-Gly),  2-(1-piperazinyl)ethylamine  glycinate  (PZEA-Gly),  and  2-(1-piperazinyl)ethylamine sarcosinate (PZEA-Sar), and their facilitation of CO2 transport by employing 13 C NMR spectroscopy. The CO2 loading and the distribution of reaction products, i.e., carbamate and bicarbonate products, were analyzed and quantified for each AAS sample. The 13 C NMR analysis revealed that PZEA-Sar had a greater CO2 loading and favored the more efficient bicarbonate pathway in comparison to PZ-Gly and PZEA-Gly. In addition, we noted marked differences in the formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products when the CO2 partial pressure and temperature  were  adjusted.  The  concentration  gradient  of  the  reaction  products  formed  indicated  that  the diffusion  of  carbamate products was the most influential factor in CO2  transport under reduced CO2  partial pressure. However, as the partial pressure of CO2  increased, bicarbonate products began to play a more significant role. At elevated temperatures, a lower absorption of CO2 was observed, resulting in the reduced generation of both reaction products. This suggests that the enhanced CO2  permeance of FTMs is mainly attributed to the accelerated diffusion of the reaction products. The findings of this study contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the transport mechanism by which AAS carriers enhance CO2 facilitation, allowing for the refinement of more efficient FTM designs.

texts.orig: In 2021, electricity generation in the United States accounted for 1551 million  metric  tons  [3]  of  CO2 emissions,  representing  32%  of  all energy-related emissions. Coal and natural gas were the primary contributors to these emissions, accounting for 59% and 40%, respectively [4]. The IPCC has highlighted the urgent need to reduce carbon dioxide emissions from existing coal- and natural gas-fired power plants through the implementation of carbon capture and storage (CCS) technologies. Retrofitting these facilities with CCS is a critical step to meeting the demands outlined in their scenarios. However, it is important to note that  CCS  technologies  still  require  further  development  prior  to commercialization, as they currently entail significant investment and energy costs, with the most costly aspect being the capture of carbon dioxide [5].

texts.orig: T.-Y. Chen and W.S.W. Ho generation infrastructures. Currently, the only commercially available technology  for  PCCC  involves  the  use  of  chemical  absorption  with amine-based  solvents  [6].  Research  and  development  efforts  are ongoing to identify alternative and cost-effective methods for CO2 separation, including the use of new materials such as non-aqueous solvents [7],  physical  and  chemical  adsorbents  [8],  ionic  liquids  [9],  carbon molecular sieves [10], advanced cryogenic processes [11], and polymeric membranes [12]. The membrane-based system is acknowledged for its economic benefits, which include a smaller physical footprint and a lower parasitic energy consumption compared to the absorption process. In order for a standalone membrane process to be viable for PCCC, it  is  essential  to  achieve  a  CO2/N2  selectivity of 140 and a CO2  permeance of more than 700 GPU (1 GPU = 10 6 cm 3 (STP) cm 2 s 1 cmHg 1 ) [13]. Amine-containing  facilitated  transport  membranes (FTMs)  have  gained  significant  attention  as  potential  solutions  for stand-alone processes due to their improved performance in terms of CO2/N2 separation in comparison to traditional polymeric membranes [14].

Research  and  development  efforts  are ongoing to identify alternative and cost-effective methods for CO2 separation, including the use of new materials such as non-aqueous solvents [7],  physical  and  chemical  adsorbents  [8],  ionic  liquids  [9],  carbon molecular sieves [10], advanced cryogenic processes [11], and polymeric membranes [12]. The membrane-based system is acknowledged for its economic benefits, which include a smaller physical footprint and a lower parasitic energy consumption compared to the absorption process. In order for a standalone membrane process to be viable for PCCC, it  is  essential  to  achieve  a  CO2/N2  selectivity of 140 and a CO2  permeance of more than 700 GPU (1 GPU = 10 6 cm 3 (STP) cm 2 s 1 cmHg 1 ) [13]. Amine-containing  facilitated  transport  membranes (FTMs)  have  gained  significant  attention  as  potential  solutions  for stand-alone processes due to their improved performance in terms of CO2/N2 separation in comparison to traditional polymeric membranes [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The superior performance of amine-containing FTMs is attributed to the  amine-mediated  transport  mechanism,  which  involves  reversible reactions between CO2 and amine carriers [15]. FTMs typically consist of two types of reactive components that act as CO2 carriers, based on their molecular weights. These types include fixed-site carriers, which are amine-bearing polymers, and mobile carriers, which are small amine molecules dispersed within the polymer matrix [16]. Upon interaction with the specified carriers, CO2 molecules undergo a chemical reaction, resulting in the formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products. These products subsequently diffuse through the membrane, traversing from the feed side to the permeate side. However, non-reactive gases, such as nitrogen (N2), are restricted to following the slower solution-diffusion mechanism.  In  particular,  the  utilization  of  mobile  carriers  in  FTMs provides a viable alternative to CO2 transport via a vehicular mechanism that significantly improves CO2 permeation [14]. Recently, amino acid salts (AASs) have generated considerable interest as mobile carriers in research as they demonstrate an impressive capability to transport CO2 across and negligible volatility loss from the membrane [17].

These types include fixed-site carriers, which are amine-bearing polymers, and mobile carriers, which are small amine molecules dispersed within the polymer matrix [16]. Upon interaction with the specified carriers, CO2 molecules undergo a chemical reaction, resulting in the formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products. These products subsequently diffuse through the membrane, traversing from the feed side to the permeate side. However, non-reactive gases, such as nitrogen (N2), are restricted to following the slower solution-diffusion mechanism.  In  particular,  the  utilization  of  mobile  carriers  in  FTMs provides a viable alternative to CO2 transport via a vehicular mechanism that significantly improves CO2 permeation [14]. Recently, amino acid salts (AASs) have generated considerable interest as mobile carriers in research as they demonstrate an impressive capability to transport CO2 across and negligible volatility loss from the membrane [17].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent research has shown that various inorganic potassium salts of amino acids have the potential to act as effective mobile carriers. In a study  conducted  by  Chen  et  al.,  potassium,  lithium,  and  piperazine glycinate salts were found to be promising mobile carriers, exhibiting CO2/N2 selectivity greater than 170 and CO2 permeance exceeding 600 GPU at a concentration of 50 wt% [18]. Moreover, Dai et al. reported that the addition of potassium prolinate (Pro-K) to the membrane led to an increased CO2 permeance, with 40 wt% Pro-K achieving a CO2 permeance of 791 GPU, almost twice that of the neat PVA membrane. The authors also observed that an increase in mobile carrier content results in an enhancement of CO2 permeance. A subsequent study revealed that utilizing an ultrahigh molecular weight polyvinylamine as a polymer matrix allows for the incorporation of up to 85 wt% of PZ-Gly without negatively impacting membrane stability [19].

The authors also observed that an increase in mobile carrier content results in an enhancement of CO2 permeance. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Han et al. further demonstrated that the 2-(1-piperazinyl)ethylamine salts of glycine and sarcosine (PZEA-Gly and PZEA-Sar) resulted in even greater permeance at 57 ◦ C, with up to 975 GPU in CO2 permeance and 163 in CO2/N2 selectivity when compared to PZ-Gly [20]. This research was further validated by the fabrication  of spiral-wound (SW) membrane modules [21] and a successful field test of the SW modules containing 85 wt% PZEA-Sar at the National Carbon Capture Center (NCCC) in Wilsonville, Alabama [22]. The module demonstrated stable performance with CO2 permeance of 1450 GPU and CO2/N2 selectivity of 185 at 67 ◦ C under actual coal-derived flue gas conditions with feed and permeate pressures of 4 and 0.3 atm, respectively, for a duration of 500 h. Zhang et al. also reported on the improved efficacy of PZEA salts of other amino acids, including alanine, lysine, and proline [23]. Janakiram et al. exhibited  the  potential  of  porous  graphene  oxide  (pGO) embedded  with  amino  functionalities  as  nanofillers  to  enhance  the separation properties and durability of thin-film composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to acquire a comprehensive understanding of the thin-film composite FTM system, additional studies were conducted to examine the role of AAS mobile carriers on CO2-facilitated transport, as well as to develop  models  for  facilitated  transport  analysis.  The  research  conducted by Deng et al. [26] found that the incorporation of AAS mobile carriers resulted in a significant enhancement of water uptake in the membrane, leading to an accelerated diffusion rate of reaction products. Additionally,  by  utilizing  molecular  dynamic  simulations,  the  study concluded that reaction kinetics and CO2 absorption capability are the key factors in the selection of a mobile carrier for the development of high-CO2 permeance FTMs. Han and Ho [27] conducted an analysis of CO2  transport in FTMs and observed that the permeance of the membrane  was  mainly  controlled  by  the  diffusion  of  amine-CO2  reaction products. It was noted that the amine-CO2 reaction in the FTM occurred over a shorter timescale than the diffusion of reaction products, indicating that reaction equilibrium is achieved throughout the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  aforementioned  findings  suggest  that  when  evaluating  the effectiveness of AAS mobile carriers, CO2 loading should be prioritized over  reaction  kinetics.  These  results  highlight  the  need  for  further research on the amine-CO2  reaction chemistry of AAS mobile carriers and  the  effect  of  reaction  conditions  on  CO2 absorption  properties, especially for FTMs containing a high content of AAS mobile carriers (up to 85%).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we examined the CO2  absorption and the amine-CO2 reaction chemistry of three AASs in relation to pressure and temperature effects by employing 13 C NMR spectroscopy. These AASs include PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly,  and  PZEA-Sar  (as  shown  in  Fig.  1),  which  have  been demonstrated as effective mobile carriers in FTMs [20]. The objective of this  research  was  to  investigate  the  effects  of  reaction  conditions, namely, CO2 partial pressure and temperature, on CO2 loading and reaction product formation with respect to the chemical structures of these AASs. The findings of this study provide valuable insights into the CO2 capture of AASs, which can aid in the development of advanced AAS carriers for FTMs.

texts.orig: All chemicals used in this experiment were of reagent grade and were utilized  as  received  without  undergoing  any  additional  purification processes.  Piperazine  (PZ,  99%),  2-(1-piperazinyl)ethylamine  (PZEA, 99%), glycine (Gly, 99%), and deuterium oxide (heavy water, D2O, 99.9 atom% D) were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Sarcosine (Sar; 98%, Acros Organics) was purchased from Fisher Scientific Inc. (Pittsburgh, PA). Pre-purified CO2 (U.S.P. grade) and argon (99.998%) were acquired from Praxair Inc. (Danbury, CT). In-house nitrogen with a purity of ≥ 99.99% was used.

texts.orig: The gas mixture was attained by combining the two gas streams of CO2 and N2, which were monitored by two mass flow controllers (Alicat Scientific,  Tucson,  AZ).  The  pressure  inside  the  absorber  device  was balanced with the external pressure at 1 atm. The duration of CO2/N2 gas  absorption  was  systematically  increased  from  2  h  to  72  h,  with variations depending on the concentration of CO2  present in the gas stream, to ensure the attainment of equilibrium between AAS and CO2. The partial pressure of CO2 in the gas mixture in contact with the AAS solution was calculated by subtracting the saturated water vapor pressure of the AAS solution from the total atmospheric pressure of 1 atm and multiplying by the mol% of CO2 in the gas mixture on the dry basis. Once the absorption was completed, the AAS solution was immediately cooled in a water bath to room temperature to allow for the quantitative 13 C NMR characterization. It was assumed that the gas mixture introduced into the AAS solution was in a state of saturation with regard to water vapor during the absorption process. The experimental setup and procedures for determining the saturated water vapor pressure using a manometer are detailed in Fig. S2 in the SI.

texts.orig: The 13 C NMR spectra of CO2-loaded AAS solutions were acquired using a Bruker Avance III HD Ascend 600 MHz spectrometer at room temperature. The quantitative spectra were obtained using the inverse gated proton decoupling method with the TopSpin software version 3.6. A total of 64 scans were taken for each NMR sample with a pulse interval of  30  s.  D2O  was  used  as  a  standard  in  a  coaxial  insert  to  achieve deuterium locking and shimming. The insert was placed in an NMR tube containing the CO2-loaded AAS solution. The NMR tube (5 mm, 600 MHz 7 ″ Select Series ™ ,  NORELL ® ) and the coaxial insert (NI5CCI -B, NORELL ® ) were purchased from Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Inc. (Andover, MA).

texts.orig: The reaction products were quantified using the integration ratios of the carbons in the reaction products and the AAS in the 13 C NMR spectra. Prior to conducting quantitative 13 C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments, spin-lattice relaxation time (T1) measurements were carried out for the AAS samples sorbed with CO2. The quantitative spectra were acquired using a pulse interval of 30 s, which was five times greater than the longest T1 observed for the carbon peaks of the CO2-sorbed AAS samples, in order to mitigate the nuclear Overhauser effect (NOE).

texts.orig: The CO2  permeance of a FTM containing a high content of AAS is affected by the CO2 loading (solubility) by the AAS in the membrane and transmembrane diffusion of the reaction products of AAS and CO2. The present study aimed to explore the reaction chemistry of AASs with CO2 in  terms  of  pressure  and  temperature  effects  on  CO2 loading  and preferred reaction pathways under flue gas conditions. For the purpose of  the  investigation,  three  amine-based AASs, PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEA-Sar, were selected as they have been previously demonstrated to be effective mobile carriers in FTMs [26]. The AAS solution was prepared at a concentration of 2 M to closely resemble the AAS concentration found in FTMs, thus providing more representative results of the reaction chemistry in FTMs as previously reported in literature [26]. The absorption temperature was controlled at 57 ◦ C, which is the typical temperature of flue gas leaving the desulfurizing unit of a coal-fired power plant [28].

texts.orig: illustrated  in  Fig.  2  (b)  and  (c),  respectively.  The  difference  in  CO2 loading can be attributed to the more effective amine functionalities (pKa value > 6) [32 -34] present in PZEA, with pKa values of 9.63, 8.51, and 4.3, compared to PZ with pKa values of 9.75 and 5.36. A higher pKa value indicates a stronger basicity of the amine group. For pKa > 6, the amine functionalities tend to favor the bicarbonate pathway over the carbamate  pathway,  offering  a  higher  CO2 loading  capacity  as  the former needs only one amine group per molecule of CO2  whereas the latter requires two amine groups for one molecule of CO2. As a result, the overall  CO2  absorption  performance  is  enhanced,  leading  to  a  more effective CO2 capture. Hence, PZEA is more effective in contributing to CO2 loading than PZ. In other words, the increased presence of stronger amine functionalities for PZEA enables it with a greater CO2 loading.

texts.orig: When evaluating the CO2 loadings of solely PZEA-based AASs, i.e., PZEA-Gly and PZEA-Sar, it is observed that PZEA-Sar displayed a similar relative CO2 loading to PZEA-Gly at lower CO2 partial pressures. However, as the CO2 partial pressure increased above approximately 0.2 atm, PZEA-Sar exhibited a higher CO2  loading capacity with the disparity growing more noticeable as the CO2  partial pressure continued to increase. The enhanced CO2 loading of PZEA-Sar was due to the increased formation of bicarbonate products, as shown in Fig. 2 (c). The higher CO2  loading and preference for the bicarbonate pathway of PZEA-Sar can be explained by the effect of steric hindrance [29]. As an analog of  Gly  with  increased  steric  hindrance  due  to  an  additional  methyl group, Sar demonstrated a reduced competitive Lewis base activity towards CO2  for the formation of carbamate products, leading to an increase in the formation of bicarbonate products. This result aligns with the more stable bicarbonate product of PZEA-Sar predicted by computational calculations [26].

texts.orig: In addition to the impact of the AAS structure on its reactivity towards CO2, as previously discussed, our results also indicate that the CO2 loading is dependent on the CO2 partial pressure. This is attributed to the equilibrium shift in the reversible reaction pathways between the amine and CO2. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a) -(c), it can be observed that as the CO2 partial pressure increased, the CO2  loading also escalated for all three AASs. However, the trends in the formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products, as shown in Fig. 2 (b) and (c), respectively, varied with the CO2 partial pressure.

As depicted in Fig. 2 (a) -(c), it can be observed that as the CO2 partial pressure increased, the CO2  loading also escalated for all three AASs. However, the trends in the formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products, as shown in Fig. 2 (b) and (c), respectively, varied with the CO2 partial pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specifically, for AASs following the carbamate pathway, the carbamate product increased significantly at low CO2 partial pressures ( < 0.2 atm) with increasing CO2  partial pressure. As the CO2  partial pressure surpassed 0.2 atm, the amount of carbamate product formed changed slowly and reached a maximum value for PZ-Gly (0.685 mol/mol AAS) and PZEA-based AASs (0.860 mol/mol AAS). On the other hand, for AASs  following  the  bicarbonate  pathway,  the  bicarbonate  product increased steadily across the range of tested pressures. Additionally, it is noted that a significantly greater proportion of amine groups in the AASs participated in carbamate formation compared to bicarbonate formation. Nearly all reacted amine groups (~97%) were involved in carbamate formation under low CO2 concentrations ( < 1 mol% CO2), whereas less than one fifth of amine groups participated in bicarbonate formation at elevated CO2 partial pressures. This concordance with the theoretical calculation results suggests that AAS carriers promote CO2 transport in the corresponding FTMs through the formation of carbamates as the preferred products [26].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As previously reported, the majority of the reaction products were identified as carbamate ions, indicating that carbamate derivatives play a crucial role in CO2 transport in FTMs. In this section, we will examine the various carbamate products produced by the three AASs and explore how variations in the chemical structure of AASs impact the formation of carbamate  derivatives  under  varying  levels  of  CO2 partial  pressure. Fig. 3 (a) -(c) present the distribution of carbamate products in the CO2loaded solutions of 2 M PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEA-Sar, respectively, as a function of CO2  partial pressure at a temperature of 57 ◦ C. The chemical structures of carbamate products produced by the AASs are depicted in Fig. 4. The AASs examined in this study possess multiple amine nucleophiles that can react with CO2  to form carbamates. The formation of carbamates can occur on one or two different nitrogen sites, leading to the production of various mono-carbamate or di-carbamate products.  The  reaction  between  CO2 and  primary  and  secondary amino groups can generate carbamate products, while CO2 cannot react with  tertiary  amino  groups  directly  and  can  only  form  bicarbonate products.

In contrast, the proportion of di-carbamate products was relatively low, varying between 0 and 2.7 mol% for PZ-Gly, 5.5 -11.0 mol% for PZEA-Gly, and 10.8 -20.6 mol% for PZEA-Sar, depending on the  CO2 partial  pressure.  When  comparing  the  formation  of  dicarbamate products in PZEA-Gly and PZEA-Sar, it was observed that PZEA-Sar had a higher occurrence. Additionally, it was found that PZEAbased AASs had a higher formation of di-carbamate products compared to PZ-Gly and that the amount of PZEA di-carbamate increased with an increase in the CO2 partial pressure [35]. This suggests that the N -methyl substituent on Sar leads to a significant decrease in the stability of mono-carbamate products, promoting a greater preference for the more efficient bicarbonate pathway in PZEA-Sar. This enhanced CO2 absorption capability makes PZEA-Sar a more effective option for CO2  separation  from  pressurized  flue  gas  streams  in  comparison  to PZEA-Gly. This observation is consistent with previously reported variations in overall carbamate formation between PZ-Gly and PZEA-Sar.

texts.orig: 3.5. Amine-CO2 reaction and CO2 permeance in thin-film FTMs
texts.text: 3.5. Amine-CO2 reaction and CO2 permeance in thin-film FTMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the previous sections, we conducted thorough analysis of the reaction products formed by three AASs (PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEASar) with CO2  and evaluated the impact of their chemical structures on the predominant formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products. We  also  examined  the  correlation  between  the  formation  of  these products and variations in CO2 partial pressures and temperatures. This section aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the role of AASs in facilitating CO2 transport through FTMs and to offer an explanation for the observed characteristics of membrane transport performance [20,37].
texts.text: In the previous sections, we conducted thorough analysis of the reaction products formed by three AASs (PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEASar) with CO2  and evaluated the impact of their chemical structures on the predominant formation of carbamate and bicarbonate products. We  also  examined  the  correlation  between  the  formation  of  these products and variations in CO2 partial pressures and temperatures. This section aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the role of AASs in facilitating CO2 transport through FTMs and to offer an explanation for the observed characteristics of membrane transport performance [20,37].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FTMs were synthesized by using high-molecular-weight copolymer, PNVFco -VAm, containing formamide/amino groups covalently bound to the polymer backbone as the fixed-site carrier and an AAS as the mobile carrier. As both materials contain hydrophilic amine functional  groups,  when  the  air-dried  membrane  was  tested  in  the  gas permeation  unit  at  57 ◦ C  and  100%  relative  humidity,  it  absorbed moisture and swelled. Based on the analysis of one of FTMs (containing 85 wt% PZEA-Sar), the AAS mobile carrier concentration was found to be ~ 2 M in the membrane under the testing conditions [38]. The water sorption capacity of the selective layer was also investigated in a previous study by our group, and the results showed that there was sufficient  water  to  participate  directly  in  the  bicarbonate  pathway  as  a reactant [26].
texts.text: The FTMs were synthesized by using high-molecular-weight copolymer, PNVFco -VAm, containing formamide/amino groups covalently bound to the polymer backbone as the fixed-site carrier and an AAS as the mobile carrier. As both materials contain hydrophilic amine functional  groups,  when  the  air-dried  membrane  was  tested  in  the  gas permeation  unit  at  57 ◦ C  and  100%  relative  humidity,  it  absorbed moisture and swelled. Based on the analysis of one of FTMs (containing 85 wt% PZEA-Sar), the AAS mobile carrier concentration was found to be ~ 2 M in the membrane under the testing conditions [38]. The water sorption capacity of the selective layer was also investigated in a previous study by our group, and the results showed that there was sufficient  water  to  participate  directly  in  the  bicarbonate  pathway  as  a reactant [26].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As referenced in Refs. [20,37], AASs were present in the high content of 85 wt% in FTMs and played a primary role in facilitating CO2 transport through the membranes. In thin-film FTMs with a selective layer thickness of 170  nm,  the  diffusion of reaction products  is the rate-determining step, with a diffusion time scale larger than that of the amine-CO2 reaction at the membrane surface [27]. Consequently, when assessing the efficacy of AAS mobile carriers, the CO2  loading factor ought to be more important than the reaction kinetics. The following paragraphs  will  evaluate  the  impact  of  AAS  structure,  pressure,  and temperature on CO2 permeance based on our findings.
texts.text: As referenced in Refs. [20,37], AASs were present in the high content of 85 wt% in FTMs and played a primary role in facilitating CO2 transport through the membranes. In thin-film FTMs with a selective layer thickness of 170  nm,  the  diffusion of reaction products  is the rate-determining step, with a diffusion time scale larger than that of the amine-CO2 reaction at the membrane surface [27]. Consequently, when assessing the efficacy of AAS mobile carriers, the CO2  loading factor ought to be more important than the reaction kinetics. The following paragraphs  will  evaluate  the  impact  of  AAS  structure,  pressure,  and temperature on CO2 permeance based on our findings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: FTMs composed of 15 wt% poly( N -vinylformamideco -vinylamine) and 85 wt% of three AASs, PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEA-Sar, exhibit CO2 permeances  that  increase  in  the  order  of  840,  910,  and  975  GPU, respectively,  and  a  CO2/N2  selectivity > 160 at 57 ◦ C as  reported  in Ref.  [20].  The  performances  were  measured  with  a  dry  feed  gas composition of 20% CO2 and 80% N2 and a sweep gas of argon with both saturated with water vapor (100% relative humidity) at 1 atm and 57 ◦ C [20], aiming to simulate the coal-derived flue gas at its typical temperature. Our findings suggest that PZEA-Sar is the most effective AAS for CO2  transport due to its N -methyl substituent on the amino group, which leads to a higher degree of steric hindrance and greater formation of  the  desirable  bicarbonate  product  than  PZEA-Gly.  Furthermore, PZEA-based  AASs  (PZEA-Gly  and  PZEA-Sar)  exhibit  a  higher  CO2 loading per mole of AAS and more stable di-carbamate products than PZ-based AASs, due to their more effective amine functionalities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the effect of CO2 partial pressure on CO2 permeance, FTMs containing AASs exhibit a non-linear increase in CO2  permeance at lower CO2 partial pressure levels [14,39]. As a result, they are ideal for CO2 enrichment from sources with low CO2 concentrations. As indicated by our results, the rising CO2  permeance with reducing CO2  partial pressure, i.e., carrier saturation phenomenon [27], can be attributed to the combined effect of two reaction pathways on CO2 loading. Under diluted CO2 partial pressure, the diffusion of carbamate products has the most significant impact on CO2 transport, as evidenced by the steep concentration gradient of carbamate products (as shown in Fig. 5 (b)). As CO2 partial pressure increases, the contribution from bicarbonate products becomes increasingly important for the membrane (as shown in Fig. 5 (c)).  As  CO2  partial  pressure increases, the concentration gradient of amine-CO2 reaction products exhibits a gradual reduction owing to the earlier  carrier  saturation  in  the  formation  of  carbamate  products  (as shown in Fig. 5 (b)) and a modest increase in the formation of bicarbonate products (as shown in Fig. 5 (c)).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, we explored the correlation between the amine-CO2 reaction chemistry and variations in CO2  partial pressure and temperature. To achieve this objective, we utilized three advanced AAS mobile carriers, namely PZ-Gly, PZEA-Gly, and PZEA-Sar, and performed 13 C NMR  spectroscopy  analysis.  The  CO2  loading  capacity  and  reaction pathway preference of the selected AASs were analyzed to determine the effect of structural modifications. The results showed that PZEA-Sar had the highest CO2 loading capacity and displayed a stronger preference for the favorable bicarbonate pathway due to its highly reactive and sterically  hindered  amine  functionalities.  Furthermore,  the  PZEA-based AASs  effectively  formed  stable  di-carbamate  products,  leading  to  a higher CO2 loading compared to PZ-Gly. The NMR analysis exhibited a clear pattern in the quantities of carbamate and bicarbonate products corresponding to the increase in the partial pressure of CO2. Given that the CO2 transport in FTMs is governed by diffusion, the results indicated that at lower CO2 partial pressures, carbamate products were predominantly responsible for CO2  transport, while an increase in CO2  partial pressure  led  to  a  more  pronounced  contribution  from  bicarbonate products. Additionally, the reduced CO2 absorption by AASs at elevated temperatures  indicates  that  the  principal  factor  contributing  to  the increased  CO2 permeance  in  FTMs  at  higher  temperatures  is  the augmented diffusion rate of the amine-CO2  reaction products. Overall, this  study has provided valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms of CO2  transport in FTMs, which can be utilized to inform the design and improvement of advanced AAS carriers.
